message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
let's proceed using helm's own procedure to implement @.helm/board/epics/001-session-foundation/ . Start with the first epic. At the end of the loop, count how much usage it all drew from my account

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- reviewer: Review code for quality, security, and performance. Use PROACTIVELY after implementing features or before merging. Runs project checks automatically. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- ui-ux-reviewer: Design and review UI/UX using Chrome MCP. Creates designs for new features and audits existing UI for visual consistency, accessibility, and UX quality. Use PROACTIVELY for design work, after UI changes, or before releases. (Tools: All tools)

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

sessions: {}

# Session foundation & shaping chats

## Goal

A story is shaped, defined, and refined from the board itself: chat sessions run as real headless
`claude` processes, structure arrives through proposal widgets instead of prose parsing, and the
adversary gate blocks Ready. This is roadmap step 1
(`.knowledge/product/roadmap.md` §Next steps); runs and review are step 2.

## Breakdown rationale

Seven stories, ordered so each ends in a demoable state and later stories ride proven
infrastructure:

1. **Session runner** stands alone first because spawning, streaming, resuming, and killing real
   CLI processes is the riskiest code in the milestone and is verifiable with no board tools.
2. **Board tools** put the in-process MCP server on top: tool calls become pending proposals,
   accepting one is the orchestrator write.
3. **Proposal widgets** are the pure-UI slice: the drawer renders the stream, the widgets, and the
   questions the first two stories produce.
4. **Shape & define chats** compose the first three into the two entry points that create cards.
   Define folds in here: same tools as shape, different seed.
5. **Refine chat** builds the brief section-by-section on the same machinery.
6. **Ready gate** adds the serial dispatcher and the cold adversary pass that blocks Ready; it
   needs finished briefs to attack, so it follows refine.
7. **Research decisions** ride the dispatcher the gate built: one more cold kind, and the shaping
   surface is complete.

Shaping context and the decisions behind this slicing:
[session-foundation](../../shaping/session-foundation.md).

7	# Session runner & kind registry
8	
9	## Goal
10	
11	The orchestrator spawns, streams, resumes, and kills a real `claude -p` session for any registered
12	session kind, with the kind fixing model, tool allowlist, system-prompt injection, and context
13	policy (`.knowledge/architecture/session-kinds.md`). Session events reach the UI live over WS.
14	
15	## Approach
16	
17	- New `src/sessions/` module, shaped like `src/board/`: pure session mechanics, no HTTP. A
18	  `src/server/` service wires it to the WS channels; `src/worker/` routes expose spawn/message.
19	- Spawn `claude -p` with `--output-format stream-json --verbose`, `--model` and the reasoning
20	  effort from the kind (the headless effort mechanism is spike-verified first,
21	  `.knowledge/architecture/claude-integration.md` §Invocation model), `--allowedTools` from the
22	  kind, `--append-system-prompt` for the kind's contract, cwd = the managed repo. Parse the
23	  NDJSON event stream (`system/init`, `assistant`, `user`, `rate_limit_event`, `result`) and
24	  forward typed events over a new WS channel in `src/shared/channels.ts`. Also pass
25	  `--include-partial-messages` (re-verify the flag against current docs) and forward its
26	  `stream_event` deltas, so the UI can render assistant output incrementally (001-03 consumes
27	  them).
28	- Capture the session id from `system/init`; later user messages resume with `--resume <id>`.
29	  Session ids persist in card frontmatter through the board store (epic/story files gain their
30	  `sessions` entry when a chat first attaches; shaping threads get theirs in 001-04, which first
31	  teaches the board module `shaping/`).
32	- Reseed on stale: a resume that exits 1 with `No conversation found` starts a fresh session
33	  seeded from the card, per the chat context policy. Steering: killing the process and resuming
34	  with a message that states the interruption.
35	- The kind registry is data: one table row per kind (model, effort, allowlist, prompt template
36	  reference, context policy), matching the registry in `session-kinds.md`. Only the chat kinds and the two
37	  cold kinds this milestone uses need entries wired to behavior.
38	- Reference mechanics: `spikes/stream-json/`; re-verify flags against current CLI docs first
39	  (`.knowledge/architecture/claude-integration.md`).
40	
41	## Blast radius
42	
43	New `src/sessions/` module (the bulk). Additions to `src/shared/channels.ts`,
44	`src/server/services/`, and `src/worker/routes/`. `src/board/` gains a frontmatter write for
45	`sessions` ids. No UI changes beyond whatever debug surface verification needs.
46	
47	## Acceptance criteria
48	
49	- [ ] Spawning a `shape`-kind session emits `system/init` carrying that kind's model, the events
50	      stream over WS as they arrive, and a tool call outside the kind's allowlist is denied
51	      (surfacing in `result.permission_denials`), never executed.
52	- [ ] A second message resumes the same session id, and the model demonstrates memory of the
53	      first turn.
54	- [ ] Killing a session mid-turn (SIGTERM) and resuming with a steering message continues with
55	      full memory.
56	- [ ] A resume against a deleted transcript fails loud and a fresh session starts, seeded from
57	      the card, with the new id persisted (exercised on a card-attached kind, `refine` on a
58	      story or `define` on an epic; shaping threads gain id persistence in 001-04).
59	- [ ] Two kinds spawn with visibly different registry rows (`shape` on Fable read-only,
60	      `research` on Sonnet) with no per-call configuration.
61	
62	## Out of scope
63	
64	- The MCP board tools and `--mcp-config` wiring (001-02).
65	- Any widget or chat UI (001-03).
66	- Run-kind compaction, the permission-prompt tool, and hooks (roadmap step 2).
67	- The queue: sessions here spawn directly on demand (001-06 adds the dispatcher).
68	
69	## Open questions
70	
71	None.
72

# Session runner & kind registry

## Goal

The orchestrator spawns, streams, resumes, and kills a real `claude -p` session for any registered
session kind, with the kind fixing model, tool allowlist, system-prompt injection, and context
policy (`.knowledge/architecture/session-kinds.md`). Session events reach the UI live over WS.

## Approach

- New `src/sessions/` module, shaped like `src/board/`: pure session mechanics, no HTTP. A
  `src/server/` service wires it to the WS channels; `src/worker/` routes expose spawn/message.
- Spawn `claude -p` with `--output-format stream-json --verbose`, `--model` and the reasoning
  effort from the kind (the headless effort mechanism is spike-verified first,
  `.knowledge/architecture/claude-integration.md` §Invocation model), `--allowedTools` from the
  kind, `--append-system-prompt` for the kind's contract, cwd = the managed repo. Parse the
  NDJSON event stream (`system/init`, `assistant`, `user`, `rate_limit_event`, `result`) and
  forward typed events over a new WS channel in `src/shared/channels.ts`. Also pass
  `--include-partial-messages` (re-verify the flag against current docs) and forward its
  `stream_event` deltas, so the UI can render assistant output incrementally (001-03 consumes
  them).
- Capture the session id from `system/init`; later user messages resume with `--resume <id>`.
  Session ids persist in card frontmatter through the board store (epic/story files gain their
  `sessions` entry when a chat first attaches; shaping threads get theirs in 001-04, which first
  teaches the board module `shaping/`).
- Reseed on stale: a resume that exits 1 with `No conversation found` starts a fresh session
  seeded from the card, per the chat context policy. Steering: killing the process and resuming
  with a message that states the interruption.
- The kind registry is data: one table row per kind (model, effort, allowlist, prompt template
  reference, context policy), matching the registry in `session-kinds.md`. Only the chat kinds and the two
  cold kinds this milestone uses need entries wired to behavior.
- Reference mechanics: `spikes/stream-json/`; re-verify flags against current CLI docs first
  (`.knowledge/architecture/claude-integration.md`).

## Blast radius

New `src/sessions/` module (the bulk). Additions to `src/shared/channels.ts`,
`src/server/services/`, and `src/worker/routes/`. `src/board/` gains a frontmatter write for
`sessions` ids. No UI changes beyond whatever debug surface verification needs.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Spawning a `shape`-kind session emits `system/init` carrying that kind's model, the events
      stream over WS as they arrive, and a tool call outside the kind's allowlist is denied
      (surfacing in `result.permission_denials`), never executed.
- [ ] A second message resumes the same session id, and the model demonstrates memory of the
      first turn.
- [ ] Killing a session mid-turn (SIGTERM) and resuming with a steering message continues with
      full memory.
- [ ] A resume against a deleted transcript fails loud and a fresh session starts, seeded from
      the card, with the new id persisted (exercised on a card-attached kind, `refine` on a
      story or `define` on an epic; shaping threads gain id persistence in 001-04).
- [ ] Two kinds spawn with visibly different registry rows (`shape` on Fable read-only,
      `research` on Sonnet) with no per-call configuration.

## Out of scope

- The MCP board tools and `--mcp-config` wiring (001-02).
- Any widget or chat UI (001-03).
- Run-kind compaction, the permission-prompt tool, and hooks (roadmap step 2).
- The queue: sessions here spawn directly on demand (001-06 adds the dispatcher).

## Open questions

None.

content.content:
1	# Runs: autonomous implementation, supervised
2	
3	A **run** is one headless Claude Code execution of a Ready story. One tap on a Ready card creates a
4	git worktree + branch (`helm/<epic>-<story>-<slug>`), then spawns the session with the story's
5	brief as the prompt and the story's permission preset attached. Everything a run needs is in the
6	brief; a run that has to ask basic scoping questions is a refinement failure, not a run problem.
7	
8	The brief is **snapshotted at spawn**: the run entry records the brief-body hash
9	([board-storage](../../architecture/board-storage.md) §Story file), compaction reseeds from the
10	snapshot ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Context management), and
11	review grades against it ([review](./review.md) §Self-grading). Editing the brief mid-run stays
12	legal, but it surfaces as a notice and takes effect on the next attempt; steering is the way to
13	redirect a live run (§Activity timeline & steering).
14	
15	## Permission presets
16	
17	Set per story, before the run:
18	
19	- **Guarded** (default): file edits auto-approved, Bash and anything destructive prompts.
20	- **Auto**: allowlisted tools run free, nothing prompts.
21	- **Manual**: everything prompts.
22	
23	The Auto allowlist is data: Helm ships the canonical list (file edits, the repo's test/lint/build
24	commands, read-only git) and a repo overrides or extends it under `.helm/`, the same
25	shared-with-local-override model templates use ([templates](../../architecture/templates.md)).
26	Init proposes the repo's own test commands into it ([init](./init.md)), and the review kind's
27	test-command Bash reads the same per-repo list
28	([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md)).
29	
30	Permission prompts surface as **approve/deny buttons on the card** (and as notifications), never
31	lost in a terminal.
32	
33	## Needs input, a first-class state
34	
35	When the agent hits a genuine decision mid-run, it calls the `ask_user` board tool and ends its
36	turn ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Board tools); the card flips
37	to Needs input ([board](./board.md)), the question renders as a quick-reply form, a notification
38	goes out ([mobile](./mobile.md)), and the answer resumes the session. The pending question is what
39	distinguishes a stuck run from a finished one when the process exits. A run never sits blocked for
40	hours because the user left their desk.
41	
42	## Activity timeline & steering
43	
44	The drawer's Activity tab streams the run live: assistant narration surfaced, tool calls collapsed
45	to one-liners, file edits as inline mini-diffs. A steering box injects a user message into the
46	running session ("stop, use the existing retry queue"); pause and stop are always available.
47	Interrupting is cheap by design: a headless process accepts no mid-run input, so the orchestrator
48	kills it and resumes the session with the steering message, and the session picks up where it left
49	off. The steering message always states that the previous action was interrupted: the resumed
50	model believes an interrupted tool call never ran, while its side effects may have partially
51	landed ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Invocation model).
52	
53	## Queue & rate limits
54	
55	Runs above the **concurrency cap** (1 in v1, default 2 once parallel runs land) queue in Ready
56	order. The board header shows a meter for the Max 5-hour window and weekly budget; the window's
57	reset clock comes from the `rate_limit_event` every session emits, while the token side is a
58	lower-bound estimate, since no API reports headroom and interactive use on other machines is
59	invisible ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Rate limits). Limit
60	errors are the authoritative signal: the queue **auto-pauses** on one instead of burning the
61	user's interactive headroom, and resumes when the window rolls. A run interrupted by a rate limit
62	pauses rather than fails: its card stays Running and the session auto-resumes with the queue.
63	Rationale: the rate-limit pool is shared with interactive use
64	([vision](../vision.md) §The constraint that shapes everything).
65	
66	Interactive chat turns bypass the queue: a human never waits behind a run. Every other kind
67	(`research`, `adversary`, `review`, `conflict`) dispatches through it with the runs
68	([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md)). An auto-pause also disables chat sends
69	and shows the reset clock, since a send during a limit only burns a failing request.
70	
71	## Run lifecycle
72	
73	A finished run commits its work on the story branch (Conventional Commits), reports its outcome
74	through the Stop hook backstop ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md)
75	§Hooks), and flips the card to Review ([review](./review.md)). A failed/aborted run parks the card
76	in Blocked with the last error surfaced; rate-limit interruptions pause instead (§Queue & rate
77	limits). Worktrees live until the story exits Review (merge or discard,
78	[board-storage](../../architecture/board-storage.md) §Worktrees).
79

# Runs: autonomous implementation, supervised

A **run** is one headless Claude Code execution of a Ready story. One tap on a Ready card creates a
git worktree + branch (`helm/<epic>-<story>-<slug>`), then spawns the session with the story's
brief as the prompt and the story's permission preset attached. Everything a run needs is in the
brief; a run that has to ask basic scoping questions is a refinement failure, not a run problem.

The brief is **snapshotted at spawn**: the run entry records the brief-body hash
([board-storage](../../architecture/board-storage.md) §Story file), compaction reseeds from the
snapshot ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Context management), and
review grades against it ([review](./review.md) §Self-grading). Editing the brief mid-run stays
legal, but it surfaces as a notice and takes effect on the next attempt; steering is the way to
redirect a live run (§Activity timeline & steering).

## Permission presets

Set per story, before the run:

- **Guarded** (default): file edits auto-approved, Bash and anything destructive prompts.
- **Auto**: allowlisted tools run free, nothing prompts.
- **Manual**: everything prompts.

The Auto allowlist is data: Helm ships the canonical list (file edits, the repo's test/lint/build
commands, read-only git) and a repo overrides or extends it under `.helm/`, the same
shared-with-local-override model templates use ([templates](../../architecture/templates.md)).
Init proposes the repo's own test commands into it ([init](./init.md)), and the review kind's
test-command Bash reads the same per-repo list
([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md)).

Permission prompts surface as **approve/deny buttons on the card** (and as notifications), never
lost in a terminal.

## Needs input, a first-class state

When the agent hits a genuine decision mid-run, it calls the `ask_user` board tool and ends its
turn ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Board tools); the card flips
to Needs input ([board](./board.md)), the question renders as a quick-reply form, a notification
goes out ([mobile](./mobile.md)), and the answer resumes the session. The pending question is what
distinguishes a stuck run from a finished one when the process exits. A run never sits blocked for
hours because the user left their desk.

## Activity timeline & steering

The drawer's Activity tab streams the run live: assistant narration surfaced, tool calls collapsed
to one-liners, file edits as inline mini-diffs. A steering box injects a user message into the
running session ("stop, use the existing retry queue"); pause and stop are always available.
Interrupting is cheap by design: a headless process accepts no mid-run input, so the orchestrator
kills it and resumes the session with the steering message, and the session picks up where it left
off. The steering message always states that the previous action was interrupted: the resumed
model believes an interrupted tool call never ran, while its side effects may have partially
landed ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Invocation model).

## Queue & rate limits

Runs above the **concurrency cap** (1 in v1, default 2 once parallel runs land) queue in Ready
order. The board header shows a meter for the Max 5-hour window and weekly budget; the window's
reset clock comes from the `rate_limit_event` every session emits, while the token side is a
lower-bound estimate, since no API reports headroom and interactive use on other machines is
invisible ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md) §Rate limits). Limit
errors are the authoritative signal: the queue **auto-pauses** on one instead of burning the
user's interactive headroom, and resumes when the window rolls. A run interrupted by a rate limit
pauses rather than fails: its card stays Running and the session auto-resumes with the queue.
Rationale: the rate-limit pool is shared with interactive use
([vision](../vision.md) §The constraint that shapes everything).

Interactive chat turns bypass the queue: a human never waits behind a run. Every other kind
(`research`, `adversary`, `review`, `conflict`) dispatches through it with the runs
([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md)). An auto-pause also disables chat sends
and shows the reset clock, since a send during a limit only burns a failing request.

## Run lifecycle

A finished run commits its work on the story branch (Conventional Commits), reports its outcome
through the Stop hook backstop ([claude-integration](../../architecture/claude-integration.md)
§Hooks), and flips the card to Review ([review](./review.md)). A failed/aborted run parks the card
in Blocked with the last error surfaced; rate-limit interruptions pause instead (§Queue & rate
limits). Worktrees live until the story exits Review (merge or discard,
[board-storage](../../architecture/board-storage.md) §Worktrees).

1	# Review: graded against the brief, not read cold
2	
3	Review inverts the usual diff-first flow: **the acceptance-criteria checklist leads, the diff
4	supports**. The user verifies verdicts instead of reading a cold 400-line diff, which is also what
5	makes review workable from a phone ([mobile](./mobile.md)).
6	
7	## Self-grading
8	
9	When a run finishes, a cheap review session (read-only allowlist plus Bash limited to the repo's
10	test commands, so evidence can include real test output) grades each acceptance criterion from the
11	run's brief snapshot ([runs](./runs.md)): **✓ / ✗ / unclear, each with evidence** (file:line
12	references, test or command output). The tally lands on the run's frontmatter entry and shows on
13	the card (5/6 ✓). Grades are claims to verify, never auto-approval; a human decides every exit.
14	(v2; v1 review is the checklist rendered ungraded + the diff. See [roadmap](../roadmap.md).)
15	
16	The checkbox follows the evidence. A criterion the session proves with a test it ran is checked on
17	the spot; one graded from code inspection alone, or covered by no automated test, stays unchecked
18	and flagged as needing a human check, with the steps to reproduce and verify it by hand
19	([board-storage](../../architecture/board-storage.md) §Story file). The review surfaces which test
20	commands ran and their output, so checked boxes carry proof and unchecked ones are the human
21	to-do list: approving is a decision about what the machine could not prove, not a re-read of the
22	whole diff.
23	
24	## Two axes: spec and standards
25	
26	Review answers two independent questions and keeps them apart. The **spec axis** is the self-grade
27	above: does the diff satisfy the brief's acceptance criteria? The **standards axis** asks the
28	orthogonal question: does the diff follow the repo's own rules (its root `CLAUDE.md` and the rules
29	Helm keeps under `.helm/agents/`) plus a baseline of common code smells, whatever the brief said? Each axis runs as its own cold session
30	([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md)) so neither pollutes the other, and the two
31	verdicts are reported side by side, never blended into one score. A change can meet every criterion
32	and still violate the repo's conventions, so a single verdict would let one hide the other.
33	Standards findings gate the exit the way the adversary gates Ready: each renders as a widget and
34	is either accepted, joining the request-changes comments for the follow-up run, or dismissed with
35	a recorded reason, and approve enables only when none stands unaddressed. Every Helm gate blocks
36	by default with a deliberate override.
37	
38	Diff tab: per-file, side-by-side, with the criteria checklist pinned above; clicking a criterion's
39	evidence jumps to the lines. Before review opens, the story branch is **rebased on the target's
40	main**; a conflict spawns an agent-assisted conflict-resolution session rather than dumping
41	conflict markers on the user. The card stays in Review behind a rebasing indicator while the
42	`conflict` session runs through the queue; a resolved rebase refreshes the diff, so the human
43	review that follows already covers the resolution, and a failed one parks the card in Blocked
44	with the error ([board](./board.md) §Status state machine).
45	
46	## Three exits
47	
48	- **Approve**: re-rebase if main moved since review opened, merge to main and push (or open a PR
49	  via `gh` in PR mode), delete worktree + branch, card → Done. Merges run one at a time once
50	  parallel runs land.
51	- **Request changes**: the user's comments (per-criterion or free-form) become the next message in
52	  the **same session, same worktree**, so full implementation context is preserved; card → Running.
53	  The follow-up runs at xhigh effort, the one escalation above the registry cap: the first attempt
54	  failing is evidence the story needs it
55	  ([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md) §Model per kind).
56	- **Discard**: worktree + branch deleted, card → Ready, runs history kept. The brief survives; only
57	  the attempt is thrown away.
58	
59	Unmet criteria are the native language of "request changes": a ✗ with the user's confirmation is
60	already a precise, testable instruction for the follow-up run. This closes the loop the product is
61	built around ([vision](../vision.md) §Wedge).

# Review: graded against the brief, not read cold

Review inverts the usual diff-first flow: **the acceptance-criteria checklist leads, the diff
supports**. The user verifies verdicts instead of reading a cold 400-line diff, which is also what
makes review workable from a phone ([mobile](./mobile.md)).

## Self-grading

When a run finishes, a cheap review session (read-only allowlist plus Bash limited to the repo's
test commands, so evidence can include real test output) grades each acceptance criterion from the
run's brief snapshot ([runs](./runs.md)): **✓ / ✗ / unclear, each with evidence** (file:line
references, test or command output). The tally lands on the run's frontmatter entry and shows on
the card (5/6 ✓). Grades are claims to verify, never auto-approval; a human decides every exit.
(v2; v1 review is the checklist rendered ungraded + the diff. See [roadmap](../roadmap.md).)

The checkbox follows the evidence. A criterion the session proves with a test it ran is checked on
the spot; one graded from code inspection alone, or covered by no automated test, stays unchecked
and flagged as needing a human check, with the steps to reproduce and verify it by hand
([board-storage](../../architecture/board-storage.md) §Story file). The review surfaces which test
commands ran and their output, so checked boxes carry proof and unchecked ones are the human
to-do list: approving is a decision about what the machine could not prove, not a re-read of the
whole diff.

## Two axes: spec and standards

Review answers two independent questions and keeps them apart. The **spec axis** is the self-grade
above: does the diff satisfy the brief's acceptance criteria? The **standards axis** asks the
orthogonal question: does the diff follow the repo's own rules (its root `CLAUDE.md` and the rules
Helm keeps under `.helm/agents/`) plus a baseline of common code smells, whatever the brief said? Each axis runs as its own cold session
([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md)) so neither pollutes the other, and the two
verdicts are reported side by side, never blended into one score. A change can meet every criterion
and still violate the repo's conventions, so a single verdict would let one hide the other.
Standards findings gate the exit the way the adversary gates Ready: each renders as a widget and
is either accepted, joining the request-changes comments for the follow-up run, or dismissed with
a recorded reason, and approve enables only when none stands unaddressed. Every Helm gate blocks
by default with a deliberate override.

Diff tab: per-file, side-by-side, with the criteria checklist pinned above; clicking a criterion's
evidence jumps to the lines. Before review opens, the story branch is **rebased on the target's
main**; a conflict spawns an agent-assisted conflict-resolution session rather than dumping
conflict markers on the user. The card stays in Review behind a rebasing indicator while the
`conflict` session runs through the queue; a resolved rebase refreshes the diff, so the human
review that follows already covers the resolution, and a failed one parks the card in Blocked
with the error ([board](./board.md) §Status state machine).

## Three exits

- **Approve**: re-rebase if main moved since review opened, merge to main and push (or open a PR
  via `gh` in PR mode), delete worktree + branch, card → Done. Merges run one at a time once
  parallel runs land.
- **Request changes**: the user's comments (per-criterion or free-form) become the next message in
  the **same session, same worktree**, so full implementation context is preserved; card → Running.
  The follow-up runs at xhigh effort, the one escalation above the registry cap: the first attempt
  failing is evidence the story needs it
  ([session-kinds](../../architecture/session-kinds.md) §Model per kind).
- **Discard**: worktree + branch deleted, card → Ready, runs history kept. The brief survives; only
  the attempt is thrown away.

Unmet criteria are the native language of "request changes": a ✗ with the user's confirmation is
already a precise, testable instruction for the follow-up run. This closes the loop the product is
built around ([vision](../vision.md) §Wedge).

content.content:
1	# Session kinds
2	
3	Every `claude` process Helm spawns belongs to one **session kind**, a closed set that fixes the
4	prompt, tool allowlist, model, and context policy for that stage of the loop. The kind is the one
5	place per-stage configuration lives, so "cheap model for read-only chat, frontier model for
6	implementation" and "compact a long run, reseed a stale chat" are table rows, not scattered flags.
7	Helm stays a single fixed workflow; the kinds are its stages, never user-authored steps
8	([vision](../product/vision.md) §Non-goals). The artifact a kind produces is stamped from a template
9	([templates](./templates.md)). The CLI mechanics each kind rides on are in
10	[claude-integration](./claude-integration.md).
11	
12	## Registry
13	
14	| Kind        | Stage                                   | Tools                                          | Model  | Effort | Context               |
15	| ----------- | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------ | ------ | --------------------- |
16	| `init`      | onboard a repo: propose scaffolding      | read-only + `propose_scaffold`                  | Fable  | high   | reseed on stale       |
17	| `shape`     | Shaping: roadmap/feature chat → epics    | read-only + `propose_epics` / `propose_stories` / `raise_decision` | Fable | high | reseed on stale |
18	| `research`  | resolve a shaping decision by investigation | read-only                                    | Sonnet | high   | always cold           |
19	| `define`    | epic → stories                           | read-only + `propose_stories`                   | Fable  | medium | reseed on stale       |
20	| `refine`    | story → brief                            | read-only + `update_brief` / `resolve_question` (+ `contest_flag` during a gate round) | Fable  | medium | reseed on stale       |
21	| `adversary` | ready gate: attack the brief             | read-only + `flag_risk`                         | Fable  | high   | always cold           |
22	| `run`       | implement a Ready story                  | permission preset + `update_card`               | Fable  | high (adaptive) | compact under pressure |
23	| `review`    | grade + test a finished run              | read-only + test-command Bash                   | Sonnet | high   | cold                  |
24	| `conflict`  | rebase conflict resolution               | worktree tools                                  | Fable  | high   | cold                  |
25	
26	`ask_user` is available to every kind, not runs alone: it is the one primitive for a session to put
27	a question to the user and end its turn (§Interaction). Read-only means the CLI's Read/Grep/Glob
28	plus the kind's board tools, with no Edit or Bash except where a row adds it.
29	
30	Chat kinds (`init`, `shape`, `define`, `refine`) spawn on the user's message and bypass the run
31	queue; every other kind dispatches through it
32	([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Queue & rate limits).
33	
34	## Model per kind
35	
36	Each kind names a model, passed as `--model`; the rate-limit pool is shared with interactive use,
37	so the cheapest model that does the job is the default ([vision](../product/vision.md) §The
38	constraint that shapes everything). **Sonnet is the floor**: Haiku is never assigned, because a
39	weak proposal or a missed fact costs more rework than the tier saves. Two models cover the
40	registry:
41	
42	- **Sonnet** (`research`, `review`): extraction and verification against something that exists:
43	  narrow factual code lookups, and criteria grading against test evidence for the human who
44	  approves. Each output is re-checked downstream or mechanically checkable, so the floor does the
45	  job. Review is re-tiered when v2 self-grading makes it autonomous
46	  ([roadmap](../product/roadmap.md)).
47	- **Fable** (`shape`, `define`, `refine`, `adversary`, `run`, `conflict`, `init`): every kind
48	  that synthesizes structure or attacks it, plus `conflict`: a rebase that applies cleanly can
49	  still drop one side's intent, no later gate re-reads the merge itself (review grades the
50	  story's criteria, not the other branch's), and the kind is rare enough that the tier costs
51	  nothing. Opus is absent by dominance, not oversight: published effort-curve benchmarks put
52	  Fable at *low* effort above Opus at its highest setting on agentic coding, at roughly half the
53	  per-task cost, because Opus barely improves with added effort while Fable climbs steeply. Any
54	  budget that affords Opus therefore affords a better Fable point. The curves are agentic-coding
55	  benchmarks; reading them onto the chat kinds is extrapolation, re-verified with the other
56	  fast-moving facts below. Opus is the recorded fallback for the synthesis kinds if Fable's
57	  subscription-inclusion terms shift, and the retry model when a run hits Fable's
58	  safety-classifier refusal (security-adjacent stories can false-positive; the CLI's headless
59	  refusal behavior is unspiked).
60	
61	**Effort is the second axis, capped at high.** Models expose reasoning-effort levels (low ·
62	medium · high · xhigh · max; the top levels vary by model): the tier sets the capability ceiling,
63	effort sets how much of it a session spends per turn. max is excluded outright; xhigh is excluded
64	for its latency and context-window burn everywhere except as `run`'s escalation lever (below).
65	How a headless spawn sets effort is unverified: spike it before building the registry
66	([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Invocation model). Below the cap, each kind sits
67	at the cheapest point that clears its quality bar, weighed by four factors: what checks the
68	output downstream, the kind's token volume, how steep the effort payoff is (Fable climbs with
69	effort; Sonnet's extraction work barely does), and interactive latency (the chat kinds bypass the
70	queue because a person is waiting, so their ceiling is a turn the user will sit through). High
71	goes where output is unchecked, stakes peak, or thoroughness is the product: `run` (highest
72	stakes and volume), `adversary` (reasoning-dense, tiny sessions), `shape` (unchecked omissions at
73	the frame, low volume), `init` (one-time), `conflict` (rare, and a failed rebase wastes a queue
74	slot), `research` (tiny volume, and a wrong finding bakes into the epic structure with no later
75	gate re-reading it), and `review` (effort buys tool-call thoroughness, and evidence is review's
76	whole product; a confidently wrong grade miscalibrates the trust the approver puts in every other
77	grade). Medium goes where a stronger stage re-checks the work: `refine` and `define`
78	(gate-checked, and Fable at medium still clears every Opus setting on the published curves). When
79	the pool binds, the tune-down lever is `run`'s effort; when quality disappoints, the tune-up
80	order is the medium cells first. One cost to watch: long-run thinking
81	fills the context window sooner, so it raises compaction pressure, the least-verified mechanic
82	([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Context management).
83	
84	**`run`'s effort adapts per story; its model never does.** The registry cell is the default, and
85	two signals move it, both riding artifacts that already exist. Refine stamps a size hint on the
86	story while writing the brief ([board-storage](./board-storage.md) §Story file); a `trivial` hint
87	drops the run to medium, safe because Fable at medium still clears every Opus setting and a wrong
88	hint costs at most one review cycle. A request-changes exit raises the follow-up run to xhigh
89	([review](../product/features/review.md) §Three exits): escalation reacts to evidence of failure,
90	which beats predicting difficulty from a brief. Dynamic model routing is rejected. The stage
91	already classifies the work, so a classifier session would spend pool tokens re-deriving a known
92	label, and its failure mode, a hard story routed to Sonnet, costs a failed run plus rework, the
93	same asymmetry that sets the Sonnet floor.
94	
95	Two resources are easy to conflate. The **rate-limit pool** is priced per model: Fable burns it
96	roughly twice as fast as Opus per token (pool weighting follows per-token price) while finishing
97	a task in fewer tokens. The **context window** burns the same per token on every tier; effort is
98	what fills it faster. Model family names are stable; the exact ids, relative pool burn, effort
99	mechanics, and Fable's subscription-inclusion terms move fast, so re-verify against current docs
100	before building on them ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md)).
101	
102	## Context policies
103	
104	Three policies cover every kind:
105	
106	- **reseed on stale** (chats). A chat resumes by session id across days or weeks. When the
107	  transcript is gone (Claude Code deletes idle ones after `cleanupPeriodDays`), the resume fails
108	  loud and the kind starts a fresh session seeded from the card
109	  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Invocation model). The user loses transcript
110	  scroll-back, never the artifact: the brief is the product
111	  ([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md)).
112	- **always cold** (`adversary`, `research`, `review`, `conflict`). These kinds never resume. Each starts fresh
113	  and reads the finished artifact with no chat history, which is the point of the adversary pass: a
114	  cold reader catches what the author and the refine chat talked themselves past.
115	- **compact under pressure** (`run`). A run is the one session long enough to exhaust its context
116	  window mid-task. Near the limit the orchestrator compacts it: progress is summarized and the
117	  session resumes with that summary plus the brief reloaded, so the contract survives
118	  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Context management).
119	
120	## Interaction
121	
122	Any session asks the user a question through `ask_user`: it records the question, the UI renders a
123	quick-reply form with a free-text fallback, and answering resumes the session with the answer. A
124	question arrives one at a time in dependency order and carries Claude's recommended answer, so the
125	user confirms or redirects, and anything the code can settle is read rather than asked
126	([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Grilling). A run that calls it flips the
127	card to Needs input ([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Needs input);
128	a chat kind renders the question inline in the drawer. This is the one interactive-question path, so
129	a mid-loop decision reaches the user the same way whichever stage raises it
130	([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Board tools).
131

- **compact under pressure** (`run`). A run is the one session long enough to exhaust its context
  window mid-task. Near the limit the orchestrator compacts it: progress is summarized and the
  session resumes with that summary plus the brief reloaded, so the contract survives
  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Context management).

## Interaction

Any session asks the user a question through `ask_user`: it records the question, the UI renders a
quick-reply form with a free-text fallback, and answering resumes the session with the answer. A
question arrives one at a time in dependency order and carries Claude's recommended answer, so the
user confirms or redirects, and anything the code can settle is read rather than asked
([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Grilling). A run that calls it flips the
card to Needs input ([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Needs input);
a chat kind renders the question inline in the drawer. This is the one interactive-question path, so
a mid-loop decision reaches the user the same way whichever stage raises it
([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Board tools).

1	# Claude CLI integration
2	
3	Every Helm conversation and run is a real Claude Code session driven through the headless CLI.
4	Facts below were verified against the official docs (code.claude.com) at project founding; the ones
5	marked spike-verified were measured against CLI 2.1.210 by the stream-json spike
6	(`spikes/stream-json/`). **Re-verify flags and auth behavior against current docs before building
7	on them**: the CLI moves fast.
8	
9	## Why the CLI, not the Agent SDK
10	
11	The Agent SDK (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`) requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`; Anthropic's docs
12	prohibit third-party products from offering claude.ai login or subscription rate limits. Headless
13	`claude -p` inherits whatever auth the local CLI has, including a Max subscription login. Helm
14	therefore shells out to the user's own logged-in CLI and stays instance-per-user. The legal page
15	(code.claude.com/docs/en/legal-and-compliance) draws the line explicitly: running Claude Code on
16	your own machine is exempt from the Consumer ToS prohibition on automated access, but third-party
17	orchestrators routing Max credentials on behalf of users are not permitted. Personal Helm sits on
18	the allowed side; a public release ships API-key auth as its distribution mode and re-checks that
19	page first ([vision](../product/vision.md) §Ambition).
20	
21	## Invocation model
22	
23	One `claude -p` process per chat turn or run segment:
24	
25	- `--output-format stream-json` (requires `--verbose` under `-p`): newline-delimited JSON events
26	  the orchestrator parses and forwards over WS. Spike-verified stream: `system/init` (session id,
27	  tools, MCP server statuses, model, permission mode, `apiKeySource: "none"` on subscription),
28	  `assistant`/`user` (full API messages with usage), `rate_limit_event` (§Rate limits), `result`
29	  (subtype `success` or `error_during_execution`, result text, usage, `permission_denials`).
30	- `--resume <session-id>`: every user message resumes the card's session; the id stays stable
31	  across resumes (spike-verified), so frontmatter stores one id per session kind
32	  ([board-storage](./board-storage.md)). Sessions survive orchestrator restarts and machine
33	  reboots.
34	- `--strict-mcp-config`: without it every headless run loads the user's global MCP servers
35	  (spike-verified); skills and settings load regardless, so runs are not hermetic. The target
36	  repo's root `CLAUDE.md` auto-loads and, through its `@.helm/agents/index.md` import, pulls in Helm's
37	  rules (plus user-global `~/.claude`), so a managed repo's standing rules already shape every Helm
38	  run; curating them is a planned feature ([roadmap](../product/roadmap.md) §Later, Rules & knowledge
39	  library).
40	- `--permission-mode` + `--allowedTools`: implement the per-story permission presets
41	  ([runs](../product/features/runs.md)); define/refine chats get the read-only allowlist
42	  (Read/Grep/Glob + board tools).
43	- `--append-system-prompt`: injects the brief template / run contract per session kind.
44	- `--model`: the per-kind model, so read-only chats stay cheap and implementation runs on the
45	  frontier model ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)).
46	- Reasoning effort accompanies the model per kind ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)). The CLI
47	  exposes effort as a session setting (`/effort`, since v2.1.205); how a headless spawn sets it
48	  (a settings key, a config pass-through, or a flag) is unverified, so spike it before building
49	  the kind registry.
50	- Working directory: the target repo (chats) or the story's worktree (runs).
51	
52	Transcripts live at `~/.claude/projects/<cwd-slug>/<session-id>.jsonl`; JSONL format is internal
53	and version-unstable, so Helm treats transcripts as Claude Code's property and keeps its own state
54	in `.helm/` frontmatter. Session lookup is scoped to the working directory (worktrees included),
55	so resume must run from the same cwd that created the session.
56	
57	Two more resume limits shape design. Claude Code deletes idle transcripts after `cleanupPeriodDays`
58	(default 30), so a session parked for weeks stops resuming: the server raises the setting, and a
59	resume that fails anyway starts a fresh session seeded from the card. The failure is loud
60	(spike-verified): exit 1, stderr `No conversation found with session ID`, one
61	`result/error_during_execution` event. Session lookup is keyed to the cwd path, not the directory
62	itself; a deleted-and-recreated worktree path still resumes (spike-verified). And a running `-p`
63	process accepts no further input: steering a live run means killing the process and resuming the
64	session with the steering message. Spike-verified: a SIGTERM mid-tool-call resumes cleanly with
65	full memory, but the resumed model believes the interrupted tool call never ran even though its
66	side effects may have partially landed, so the steering message states the interruption.
67	
68	## Context management
69	
70	Model and context are set per session kind ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)). Two mechanics
71	live at the CLI boundary. Chats reseed: a resume that fails because the transcript was cleaned up
72	starts a fresh session seeded from the card (§Invocation model). Runs compact: a headless process
73	has no interactive `/compact`, so the orchestrator watches window usage from the `assistant` and
74	`result` event totals and, near the limit, ends the turn and writes a **handoff**: the run squeezed
75	to its resumable core (what is in flight and why, and what is left to do), referencing the brief,
76	the diff, and settled decisions by path instead of restating them, with secrets redacted. The run
77	resumes from that handoff plus the brief reloaded from its spawn snapshot (a mid-run hand edit
78	never rewrites the contract, [runs](../product/features/runs.md)), and is told its earlier tool
79	output was summarized, the same caution a steering resume carries (§Invocation model). Compaction is
80	designed, not yet spike-verified: re-verify the CLI's headless context behavior before building it.
81	
82	## Board tools (in-process MCP)
83	
84	Chat sessions receive an MCP server hosted by the orchestrator (official MCP SDK, streamable HTTP
85	on localhost, mounted on the orchestrator's own Hono app), passed via `--mcp-config`. The tools
86	vary by session kind ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)): `shape` proposes epics
87	(`propose_epics`) and stories (`propose_stories`) and raises feature-level decisions
88	(`raise_decision`), `define` proposes stories, `refine` builds the
89	brief (`update_brief`, `resolve_question`) and contests gate flags (`contest_flag`,
90	[define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Ready gate), `adversary` raises blocking
91	flaws (`flag_risk`), and
92	`init` proposes repo scaffolding (`propose_scaffold`).
93	Tool calls become UI proposal widgets; **accepting a widget is what writes the board file**, the
94	tool call itself mutates nothing. This is how structure is extracted from conversation without
95	parsing prose ([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Proposal widgets).
96	
97	Run sessions get `update_card`, which applies body edits (noting decisions and progress; criteria
98	checkboxes belong to review, [review](../product/features/review.md) §Self-grading) so the agent
99	never writes `.helm/` files itself ([board-storage](./board-storage.md) §Mutation rules).
100	
101	`ask_user` is available to every session kind, not runs alone. It records a question and tells the
102	agent to end its turn; the answer resumes the session, and a pending question is what distinguishes
103	a stuck session from a finished one when the process exits ([runs](../product/features/runs.md)
104	§Needs input). A run that calls it flips the card to Needs input; a chat kind renders the question
105	inline in the drawer.
106	
107	## Permission prompts
108	
109	Runs pass `--permission-prompt-tool` naming a tool on the same orchestrator MCP server. When a
110	Guarded-preset run needs approval, the CLI calls that tool; the orchestrator holds the request,
111	pushes a WS event (approve/deny buttons on the card, plus a notification), and the tool's return
112	value allows or denies. No hook polling, no terminal prompt. Spike-verified contract: the CLI
113	calls the tool with `{tool_name, input, tool_use_id}`; text content
114	`{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":<input>}` releases the call, `{"behavior":"deny","message"}`
115	blocks it. Read-only commands (`git log`, `ls`) never consult the tool; only mutating calls ask. A
116	held approval survived 4 minutes on default env; the orchestrator still raises `MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT`
117	for longer waits, and if hour-long holds prove untenable the fallback is deny-with-reason plus a
118	Needs-input resume.
119	
120	## Hooks
121	
122	Run sessions carry hook config (via `--settings`) as a backstop: the Stop hook POSTs the run
123	outcome to the orchestrator's HTTP API, and writes the main checkout's frontmatter directly only
124	when the orchestrator is unreachable. That keeps the board truthful if the orchestrator missed the
125	stream, while preserving its single-writer rule ([board-storage](./board-storage.md) §Mutation
126	rules). The Stop hook fires on normal completion and does not fire on SIGTERM (spike-verified:
127	exit 143, no `result` event); startup reconciliation ([overview](./overview.md) §Shape) is the
128	safety net for killed processes.
129	
130	## Auth on a headless host
131	
132	`claude setup-token` (run once on a machine with a browser) mints a long-lived OAuth token tied to
133	the Max subscription; the orchestrator's environment carries it as `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`.
134	Precedence gotcha: any `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` / `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` in the environment outranks
135	the OAuth token; keep the orchestrator's env clean of them.
136	
137	## Rate limits
138	
139	Subscription limits (5-hour rolling window + weekly caps) apply identically to headless and
140	interactive use, one shared pool. Every spawned session emits a `rate_limit_event`
141	(spike-verified) carrying `status`, `resetsAt`, and the window type (`five_hour`), so the meter
142	gets the window's reset clock and an allowed/limited signal for free on each spawn. Numeric
143	headroom stays unreported, and interactive use on other machines is invisible to Helm, so the
144	token side of the meter (counts from result events, summed across every session kind: chats, runs,
145	grading, conflict resolution) is a lower-bound estimate. Limit errors are the authoritative
146	signal: the queue pauses on one and resumes when the window rolls
147	([runs](../product/features/runs.md) §Queue & rate limits).
148

# Claude CLI integration

Every Helm conversation and run is a real Claude Code session driven through the headless CLI.
Facts below were verified against the official docs (code.claude.com) at project founding; the ones
marked spike-verified were measured against CLI 2.1.210 by the stream-json spike
(`spikes/stream-json/`). **Re-verify flags and auth behavior against current docs before building
on them**: the CLI moves fast.

## Why the CLI, not the Agent SDK

The Agent SDK (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`) requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`; Anthropic's docs

`claude -p` inherits whatever auth the local CLI has, including a Max subscription login. Helm
therefore shells out to the user's own logged-in CLI and stays instance-per-user. The legal page
(code.claude.com/docs/en/legal-and-compliance) draws the line explicitly: running Claude Code on
your own machine is exempt from the Consumer ToS prohibition on automated access, but third-party
orchestrators routing Max credentials on behalf of users are not permitted. Personal Helm sits on
the allowed side; a public release ships API-key auth as its distribution mode and re-checks that
page first ([vision](../product/vision.md) §Ambition).

## Invocation model

One `claude -p` process per chat turn or run segment:

- `--output-format stream-json` (requires `--verbose` under `-p`): newline-delimited JSON events
  the orchestrator parses and forwards over WS. Spike-verified stream: `system/init` (session id,

  `assistant`/`user` (full API messages with usage), `rate_limit_event` (§Rate limits), `result`

- `--resume <session-id>`: every user message resumes the card's session; the id stays stable
  across resumes (spike-verified), so frontmatter stores one id per session kind
  ([board-storage](./board-storage.md)). Sessions survive orchestrator restarts and machine
  reboots.
- `--strict-mcp-config`: without it every headless run loads the user's global MCP servers
  (spike-verified); skills and settings load regardless, so runs are not hermetic. The target

  rules (plus user-global `~/.claude`), so a managed repo's standing rules already shape every Helm

- `--permission-mode` + `--allowedTools`: implement the per-story permission presets

- `--append-system-prompt`: injects the brief template / run contract per session kind.
- `--model`: the per-kind model, so read-only chats stay cheap and implementation runs on the
  frontier model ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)).
- Reasoning effort accompanies the model per kind ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)). The CLI
  exposes effort as a session setting (`/effort`, since v2.1.205); how a headless spawn sets it
  (a settings key, a config pass-through, or a flag) is unverified, so spike it before building
  the kind registry.
- Working directory: the target repo (chats) or the story's worktree (runs).

Transcripts live at `~/.claude/projects/<cwd-slug>/<session-id>.jsonl`; JSONL format is internal
and version-unstable, so Helm treats transcripts as Claude Code's property and keeps its own state
in `.helm/` frontmatter. Session lookup is scoped to the working directory (worktrees included),
so resume must run from the same cwd that created the session.

Two more resume limits shape design. Claude Code deletes idle transcripts after `cleanupPeriodDays`
(default 30), so a session parked for weeks stops resuming: the server raises the setting, and a
resume that fails anyway starts a fresh session seeded from the card. The failure is loud
(spike-verified): exit 1, stderr `No conversation found with session ID`, one

itself; a deleted-and-recreated worktree path still resumes (spike-verified). And a running `-p`
process accepts no further input: steering a live run means killing the process and resuming the
session with the steering message. Spike-verified: a SIGTERM mid-tool-call resumes cleanly with
full memory, but the resumed model believes the interrupted tool call never ran even though its
side effects may have partially landed, so the steering message states the interruption.

## Context management

Model and context are set per session kind ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)). Two mechanics
live at the CLI boundary. Chats reseed: a resume that fails because the transcript was cleaned up
starts a fresh session seeded from the card (§Invocation model). Runs compact: a headless process
has no interactive `/compact`, so the orchestrator watches window usage from the `assistant` and
`result` event totals and, near the limit, ends the turn and writes a **handoff**: the run squeezed
to its resumable core (what is in flight and why, and what is left to do), referencing the brief,
the diff, and settled decisions by path instead of restating them, with secrets redacted. The run
resumes from that handoff plus the brief reloaded from its spawn snapshot (a mid-run hand edit

designed, not yet spike-verified: re-verify the CLI's headless context behavior before building it.

## Board tools (in-process MCP)

Chat sessions receive an MCP server hosted by the orchestrator (official MCP SDK, streamable HTTP

vary by session kind ([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md)): `shape` proposes epics

(`raise_decision`), `define` proposes stories, `refine` builds the

[define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Ready gate), `adversary` raises blocking

`init` proposes repo scaffolding (`propose_scaffold`).
Tool calls become UI proposal widgets; **accepting a widget is what writes the board file**, the

`ask_user` is available to every session kind, not runs alone. It records a question and tells the

## Permission prompts

Runs pass `--permission-prompt-tool` naming a tool on the same orchestrator MCP server. When a

pushes a WS event (approve/deny buttons on the card, plus a notification), and the tool's return

calls the tool with `{tool_name, input, tool_use_id}`; text content
`{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":<input>}` releases the call, `{"behavior":"deny","message"}`
blocks it. Read-only commands (`git log`, `ls`) never consult the tool; only mutating calls ask. A
held approval survived 4 minutes on default env; the orchestrator still raises `MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT`
for longer waits, and if hour-long holds prove untenable the fallback is deny-with-reason plus a
Needs-input resume.

## Hooks

Run sessions carry hook config (via `--settings`) as a backstop: the Stop hook POSTs the run
outcome to the orchestrator's HTTP API, and writes the main checkout's frontmatter directly only
when the orchestrator is unreachable. That keeps the board truthful if the orchestrator missed the

exit 143, no `result` event); startup reconciliation ([overview](./overview.md) §Shape) is the

## Auth on a headless host

`claude setup-token` (run once on a machine with a browser) mints a long-lived OAuth token tied to
the Max subscription; the orchestrator's environment carries it as `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`.
Precedence gotcha: any `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` / `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` in the environment outranks
the OAuth token; keep the orchestrator's env clean of them.

## Rate limits

Subscription limits (5-hour rolling window + weekly caps) apply identically to headless and
interactive use, one shared pool. Every spawned session emits a `rate_limit_event`

content.content:
1	# Board storage: markdown in the target repo
2	
3	A board is a `.helm/` directory inside the repo it manages. Everything Helm writes into a repo lives
4	under `.helm/`: the board (`.helm/board/`), the repo's agent rules (`.helm/agents/`), its knowledge
5	base (`.helm/knowledge/`), and any template overrides. The one footprint outside `.helm/` is a single
6	line in the repo's root `CLAUDE.md`, `@.helm/agents/index.md`, which pulls Helm's rules into every
7	Claude Code session (native `@`-import, [claude-integration](./claude-integration.md)). Routing
8	through `agents/index.md` keeps a single file named `CLAUDE.md` in the repo (its own). Removing Helm
9	is deleting `.helm/` and that one line; an update touches only `.helm/`.
10	
11	Files are the truth; the orchestrator and UI are views over them. Consequences: boards are git-versioned with the code they describe,
12	hand-editable in any editor (the file watcher live-reloads the UI), and readable by the
13	implementing agent mid-run (agent writes flow through board tools, §Mutation rules). The board
14	lives in the repo's server-side main checkout: chats run there, merges land there, and switching
15	that checkout's branch swaps the board out from under the orchestrator.
16	
17	## Layout
18	
19	```
20	.helm/
21	  agents/                # agent rule files; the repo's root CLAUDE.md imports agents/index.md
22	    index.md             # single entry point; imports the glossary and rule docs below
23	    glossary.md          # ubiquitous-language glossary
24	    <topic>.md           # additional Helm-managed rule docs
25	  knowledge/             # the knowledge base (what/why docs), pulled on demand
26	    index.md             # navigation map, referenced from agents/index.md
27	  templates/             # per-repo generation-template overrides
28	  board/                 # orchestrator runtime state: watched, worktree-excluded
29	    shaping/
30	      offline-sync.md    # a roadmap thread: shape-chat session id + agreed notes
31	    epics/
32	      012-offline-sync/
33	        epic.md          # goal, epic-chat session id, breakdown rationale
34	        01-sync-engine.md  # one story = one file
35	        02-conflict-ui.md
36	```
37	
38	IDs are `<epic>-<story>` ordinal pairs (`012-01`), stable forever; slugs can be renamed, IDs
39	can't. A deleted epic or story retires its ordinal: new entries mint the next number, so a
40	reference in git history stays unambiguous.
41	
42	## Classification
43	
44	One classifier decides what each path under `.helm/board/` is; the loader and the watcher both
45	consume it, so a fresh load and a live edit never disagree. All board content lives under
46	`.helm/board/`, which holds two directories, `shaping/` and `epics/`; the rest of `.helm/`
47	(`agents/`, `knowledge/`, `templates/`) is Helm's rules, knowledge, and templates, outside the
48	board and watched by nothing. The policy, at every depth:
49	
50	- Dotfiles are ignored.
51	- Under `shaping/`, only `<slug>.md` shaping threads are valid; every other entry is invalid. A
52	  shaping thread carries a `shape` session id and the agreed roadmap notes, and its accepted
53	  proposals write new epics ([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Shaping the
54	  roadmap).
55	- Epic directories are `<NNN>-<slug>/`; every other entry directly under `epics/` is invalid.
56	- Inside an epic directory only `epic.md` and story files `<NN>-<slug>.md` are valid; every other
57	  entry (a stray file, an editor dropping like `01-x.md~`, a subdirectory) is invalid.
58	- Ordinals are unique: two directories parsing to the same epic number, or two files to the same
59	  story number in one epic, are **all** invalid — no winner is elected, so a restart shows what was
60	  live. Deleting the collision rehabilitates the survivor.
61	
62	An invalid path is dropped from the board (its content is never guessed at) and listed in the
63	invalid banner while the file exists.
64	
65	## Story file
66	
67	Frontmatter is machine state; the body is the brief, in the fixed template order.
68	
69	```markdown
70	---
71	id: 012-01
72	status: review        # backlog|refining|ready|running|needs-input|review|done|blocked
73	depends: []           # sibling story ids
74	size: trivial         # refine's size hint, optional; absent = standard
75	branch: helm/012-01-sync-engine
76	gate: { passed: 2026-07-14T18:03:00Z, brief: <hash>, overrides: ["<flag>: <reason>"] }
77	sessions: { refine: <uuid> }
78	runs:                 # one entry per implement session; request-changes follow-ups extend it
79	  - { n: 1, session: <uuid>, brief: <hash>, started: 2026-07-15T09:12:00Z, outcome: review, grades: 5/6, tokens: 184000, minutes: 22 }
80	---
81	# Sync engine
82	## Goal
83	## Approach
84	## Blast radius
85	## Acceptance criteria
86	## Out of scope
87	## Open questions
88	```
89	
90	Acceptance criteria and Open questions are `- [ ]` checklists. A checked criterion is verified:
91	the review session checks one only when automated evidence proves it (a test it ran and passed),
92	the human checks the rest at review ([review](../product/features/review.md) §Self-grading). A
93	checked question is resolved, and unresolved questions are what the ready gate counts
94	([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Ready gate).
95	
96	`size` is refine's size hint, stamped while the brief is written; a `trivial` value drops the
97	story's run to medium effort and nothing else reads it
98	([session-kinds](./session-kinds.md) §Model per kind).
99	
100	`gate` records the adversary pass: timestamp, the hash of the brief body it binds to, and the
101	dismissed flags with their override reasons; any brief edit stales it
102	([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Ready gate). A run entry records the
103	brief hash the run was spawned with (the contract review grades against,
104	[runs](../product/features/runs.md)) and, once graded, the self-grade tally the Review card
105	shows. One entry spans one implement session: request-changes follow-ups accumulate onto it, and
106	a new entry starts when discard retires the session
107	([review](../product/features/review.md) §Three exits).
108	
109	The orchestrator writes frontmatter in fixed key order (id · status · depends · size · branch ·
110	gate · sessions · runs) with one flow-styled run per line, so a rewrite diffs as exactly the lines
111	that changed.
112	
113	`epic.md` has the same shape: frontmatter holds `sessions: { define: <uuid> }` (the epic chat);
114	the body is `# Title`, the goal, and the breakdown rationale. A shaping thread under
115	`.helm/board/shaping/` holds `sessions: { shape: <uuid> }`, the agreed roadmap notes, and a Decisions
116	checklist (the
117	feature-level open questions the breakdown waits on,
118	[define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Shaping the roadmap); accepting its proposals
119	writes new epics, so it is a source of cards rather than a card.
120	
121	## Mutation rules
122	
123	- **The orchestrator is the single writer.** UI drags, accepted proposal widgets, queue/run
124	  events, and hook POSTs all land as orchestrator writes to the main checkout, so concurrent
125	  writers and read-modify-write races are designed out rather than locked around. A Stop hook
126	  writes frontmatter directly only when the orchestrator is unreachable
127	  ([claude-integration](./claude-integration.md) §Hooks).
128	- **Chat never writes board files.** Accepting a proposal widget is the single mutation path from
129	  conversation ([define-refine](../product/features/define-refine.md) §Proposal widgets).
130	- The implementing agent notes decisions and progress on its **own** card's body through the
131	  `update_card` tool, never by editing files. It never touches acceptance-criteria checkboxes
132	  (those belong to review, [review](../product/features/review.md) §Self-grading) or status;
133	  status flows through run events.
134	- **Hand edits stay legal** (files are the truth) but the watcher validates them: malformed
135	  frontmatter or an illegal status transition is surfaced in the UI and never acted on (no run
136	  spawns from a hand-typed `running`).
137	- **Deletion is the terminal move.** Dropping a story, archiving a finished epic, or clearing a
138	  spent shaping thread deletes the file or folder after an explicit confirmation; git history is
139	  the archive, so no archive directory and no `dropped` status exist.
140	
141	## Worktrees
142	
143	Worktrees live outside the repo working tree, under an orchestrator-owned directory
144	(`~/.helm/worktrees/<repo>/<story-id>/`), one per story, created at first run and deleted on
145	approve/discard ([review](../product/features/review.md) §Three exits). The story branch is the
146	durable artifact; the worktree is disposable.
147	
148	Worktrees are created with a sparse checkout that excludes the board state (`.helm/board/`): a story
149	branch never carries board changes, so story files can't conflict at
150	rebase or merge and ephemeral state (a `running` status) never enters git history through a run. The
151	rest of `.helm/` stays in the worktree, so a run still loads the repo's Helm rules through the root
152	`CLAUDE.md` import. The run reads its brief from the prompt and updates its card through board tools
153	(§Mutation rules).
